Talksmith

build a talk from what you already know

Intro — what it is, how to install it, how to use it

Author: Paulo Veiga (@veigap)
Last modified: 2026-07-14

01What problems are we addressing? 02What is Talksmith? 03The workflow in practice 04Behind the scenes 05Getting started
01

What problems are we addressing?

What problems are we addressing?

Slides are where knowledge goes to die.

The deck-first workflow throws away the expensive part.

Open cold with this line. Pause. Then: 'Who has rebuilt a talk they'd already given, from scratch, because finding the old material was harder than redoing it?' ~2 min.
What problems are we addressing?

The cost of the blank page

What starting from scratch costs, in three numbers:

6 hrstypical time to rebuild a class you already gave
~0%of last time's material you actually re-find and reuse
3×times the same source serves different classes — if you keep it
Numbers are illustrative. The third one sets up the compounding argument. ~2 min.
What problems are we addressing?

Four problems, one root cause

Scattered sources

Papers, chats, and downloads live in a dozen places; finding last time's material costs more than redoing it.

Knowledge trapped in decks

Once it's in slides, you can't query, diff, or reuse it.

No reuse across classes

Every class starts cold, even when it overlaps last semester's.

No record of decisions

Why is this slide here? Why was that one cut? The reasoning evaporates.

Four cards, one per pain — each maps to a Talksmith answer you'll show later. ~2 min.
01What problems are we addressing? 02What is Talksmith? 03The workflow in practice 04Behind the scenes 05Getting started
02

What is Talksmith?

What is Talksmith?

This is how you'll work — day to day

  • It's all chat: no new UI, no slide editor to learn
  • New or resume: start a fresh talk, or pick up one under talks/ where you left off
  • Drop & review: add sources, answer a few questions, leave feedback bullets — it does the rest
  • Always in view: the Progress and Context panels show what it's reading and doing
Live screenshot from Claude (Cowork). Point out the Progress / Context panels on the right. ~1 min.
What is Talksmith?

Not a slide generator

Deck generatorRAG chatbotTalksmith
Produces slides directlyRe-reads the raw pile each queryCompiles sources once
The deck is the artifactNothing compiled or curatedDrafts a thesis-first outline
Nothing reusable next timeNo durable outlineRenders slides on demand
Land on the last column: same inputs, completely different thing to own afterward. ~3 min.

What is Talksmith?

“

Instead of re-reading raw documents on every request, compile them once into a persistent, cross-linked knowledge base — the way source code compiles once and runs efficiently thereafter.

— on Andrej Karpathy's "LLM wiki" pattern

Let the quote sit. This is the conceptual license for everything Talksmith does. ~1 min.
What is Talksmith?

The three layers

  • Raw sources: immutable ground truth the agent reads but never rewrites
  • The wiki: synthesized, cross-linked Markdown the agent owns and keeps updated
  • Projections: final.md, then HTML or .pptx — rendered on demand and disposable
Walk left to right once, then stop and let the diagram breathe. Then: 'I drew that as rough ASCII — Talksmith rendered the SVG.' ~3 min.
What is Talksmith?

Your diagrams draw themselves

You don't draw diagrams Talksmith proposes an outline version of each diagram as ASCII right in your draft — edit it if you want — then renders it to a clean, styled SVG at Polish. No more time spent making diagrams look nicer. Every diagram in this talk was generated that way.

The single most surprising feature for newcomers — give it its own beat. ~2 min.
01What problems are we addressing? 02What is Talksmith? 03The workflow in practice 04Behind the scenes 05Getting started
03

The workflow in practice

The workflow in practice

Eight steps, driven from chat

  • You talk to Talksmith in chat; it keeps draft.md / final.md as the single source of truth.
  • The live HTML preview and the final render are optional extras.
This image is the plugin's own workflow diagram — a Markdown image ref, used verbatim. ~3 min.
The workflow in practice

Draft your way

Interview

Talksmith asks; you answer.

Agent Draft

It drafts from your corpus, then asks.

Presenter Outline

You give titles; it fills them.

Most people start with Interview and graduate to Agent Draft once the corpus is rich. ~2 min.
The workflow in practice

Review is a loop

You refine draft.md in rounds — leave inline feedback, it's applied, you review again, until the talk is right.

  • 1 · Leave feedback: drop plain-text bullets right in draft.md, under each slide's Presenter feedback — as many as you want, in any editor
  • 2 · Talksmith applies: it stamps each bullet, makes the change, and closes it with a one-line resolution kept as an audit trail
  • 3 · Review and repeat: look again, add more feedback, go around once more — the loop runs as many rounds as it takes until you say "final"

Every round is about the content, not how it looks — visuals and styling come later at Polish.

Meta-moment: open this talk's draft.md and show the real feedback bullets. ~4 min.
The workflow in practice

What you end up with

draft.md

The working outline you edit, with its feedback audit trail.

final.md

The polished, delivery-ready derivative — diagrams rendered, feedback stripped.

research/corpus/

The compiled knowledge base backing every claim.

memory.md

The progress log that lets you stop and resume anytime.

draft.md survives Polish untouched — final.md is always re-derivable. ~2 min.
The workflow in practice

One source, three outputs

The same final.md renders to any of these — pick per audience:

HTML deck

a self-contained Reveal.js deck — present in the browser or share a link.

PowerPoint

a native .pptx (strict or free-form) for Keynote / PowerPoint.

PDF

export the HTML deck to PDF for handouts.

You're looking at one right now — this deck was rendered from its draft.md with Talksmith.

Tie back to 'slides are a projection': one source, many deliverables — and you're viewing the HTML output live. ~1 min.
01What problems are we addressing? 02What is Talksmith? 03The workflow in practice 04Behind the scenes 05Getting started
04

Behind the scenes

Behind the scenes

Five roles, one source of truth

Librarian

Restructures raw sources into a uniform corpus. Preserves; doesn't compress.

Composer

Reviews slides against thesis, audience, sources, and learned rules.

Editor

The muscle: writes draft.md, applies feedback, produces final.md.

Illustrator

Turns your ASCII into styled SVGs at Polish.

Global-Librarian

Curates finalized Talks into the shared knowledge library.

You never dispatch these by hand — the orchestrator does, and narrates in plain outcomes. ~3 min.
Behind the scenes

It's Markdown all the way down

Diffable

Every change shows up in git diff.

Portable

The same outline renders to HTML or .pptx.

Round-trippable

Edited the deck in Keynote? Reconcile it back into draft.md.

The reverse path is optional — you only need it when you've edited the deck outside the cycle (e.g. tweaked the .pptx in PowerPoint).

The round-trip is what makes Markdown-as-source safe: you never lose downstream edits. ~3 min.
Behind the scenes

Markdown vs. a binary deck

Pros

Diffable, versionable, greppable · Portable across any renderer · Outlives any single tool

Cons

Opaque to version control · Locked to one application · Knowledge trapped in the format

Binary decks are more WYSIWYG — but that's the projection's job, not the source's. ~2 min.
Behind the scenes

One base, many decks

Four renders of the same talk — the projection point, made visual. ~2 min.
Behind the scenes

Knowledge that compounds

1

knowledge base per subject that outlives every renderer, every semester, and every teammate handoff.

The hero metric of the whole talk. Say it, then move to close. ~1 min.
Behind the scenes

A semester, compounding

Class 1

Corpus seeded from three papers; first outline drafted.

Class 3

Alice's indexed sources answer Bob's new questions — zero re-reading.

Class 7

Recurring feedback promoted to learnings.md; every future class inherits it.

Next term

A new teammate clones the repo and is productive on day one.

The emotional payoff of 'compounds across classes and co-teachers.' ~2 min.

05

Getting started

Setup, once

Quick breath before the live demo. ~15 sec.
Getting started

Three commands

/talksmith:init writes exactly one file — a thin CLAUDE.md stub. Commit and push it; teammates who clone the repo are set up automatically.

# Install the plugin (once per machine), inside a Claude Code session
/plugin marketplace add veigap/talksmith
/plugin install talksmith@talksmith

# Scaffold your subject repo
/talksmith:init

# Open a fresh session in that repo and say:
Hi Talksmith
Do this live if there's a projector. ~4 min.
Getting started

From nothing to running

1

Create

one Git repo per subject — the shared home for its material

2

Install

the plugin, once per machine, from the marketplace

3

Initialize

/talksmith:init drops the CLAUDE.md stub; commit it

4

Start

open a session, say "Hi Talksmith", and follow the workflow

Steps 1–3 happen once per repo; step 4 every session. ~2 min.
Getting started

What lives in the repo

profile.md

subject, audience, duration, language — set once, inherited by every class

learnings.md

the team's editorial taste, grown from recurring feedback

logo.*

your institution logo; a neutral placeholder if you skip it

talks/

one folder per class; where the corpus and outlines accumulate

knowledge-library/

the curated cross-Talk topic index shared by the team

This folder is where the compounding lives. ~3 min.
Getting started

Quick check

You render a .pptx, then tweak it in PowerPoint. What's the source of truth?

  1. The .pptx you just edited
  2. final.md
  3. draft.md
Answer

draft.md

The deck is a projection; edits made downstream are reconciled back into draft.md, so the next Polish re-derives final.md.

Let the room vote by hands before the reveal. The aha: even PowerPoint edits flow back to the Markdown. ~2 min.

Key takeaways

Stop rebuilding talks from scratch.

Build a knowledge base that compounds, and let the deck be a disposable projection of it.
Three commands to start; chat and draft.md to run; a closed-forever audit trail for every decision.
The diagrams even draw themselves.
Mirror the opening hook: the blank page is a choice, and you can stop choosing it. ~2 min.

Try it today

Install

/plugin marketplace add veigap/talksmith

Pick a subject

Make the one you teach most your first repo.

Questions?

Let's talk.

Leave the install command on screen during Q&A. ~1 min to deliver.
Build a talk from what you already know.
Full-bleed closing line. Hold it into Q&A. ~15 sec.
